Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.

- World model: Player (white pixel) moved from [45,36] to [42,36] - that's 3 cells UP per press, not 1. The blue cross may be a movement track/rail. Need to understand movement mechanics better. Goal: Likely navigate to collect targets or reach the yellow cross.
- Plan: Test movement in other directions to understand the movement pattern, then plan a route to targets.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The player moved from [42, 36] to [42, 39] - that's 3 cells RIGHT. So the movement is consistently 3 cells per direction.
Current player position: [42, 39]
Let me now map out the reachable positions. Starting from [45, 36], moving in 3-cell increments:
- Row positions: 45, 42, 39, 36, 33, 30, 27, 24, 21, 18, 15, 12, 9, 6, 3, 0
- Col positions: 36, 39, 42, 45, 48, 51, 54, 57, 60, 63 (right) and 33, 30, 27, 24, 21, 18, 15, 12, 9, 6, 3, 0 (left)
Blue targets:
- [16, 48] - row 16 not on grid (15, 18 are), col 48 is on grid
- [24, 40] - row 24 is on grid, col 40 not on grid (39, 42 are)
- [24, 53] - row 24 is on grid, col 53 not on grid (51, 54 are)
- [35, 48] - row 35 not on grid (33, 36 are), col 48 is on grid
Hmm, the targets don't align with the 3-cell grid. Maybe the cross needs to OVERLAP with targets, not have its center exactly on them. Or maybe SPACE does something.
Let me think about this differently. The cross has arms extending from the center. The cross is about 13 pixels in each direction from center. So if the center is at [42, 39], the cross covers:
- Vertical: rows ~29 to 55 at col 39
- Horizontal: cols ~26 to 52 at row 42
The blue target at [35, 48] - is it within the cross's reach? Row 35 is within 29-55, col 48 is within 26-52. So maybe the cross can "collect" targets that fall within its area?
But wait, the targets are gray squares with colored centers. They're 3x3 squares (8 pixels for the gray border + 1 for the center). Let me think about whether the cross overlaps with any targets currently.
Actually, I should try pressing SPACE to see what it does. Maybe SPACE is the "collect" or "interact" action.
Let me first try to navigate to a target and then press SPACE. But first, let me understand the grid better.
Actually, let me try a different approach. Let me see if the cross can overlap with targets. The closest blue target to the current position [42, 39] would be... let me calculate distances:
- [16, 48]: |42-16| + |39-48| = 26 + 9 = 35
- [24, 40]: |42-24| + |39-40| = 18 + 1 = 19
- [24, 53]: |42-24| + |39-53| = 18 + 14 = 32
- [35, 48]: |42-35| + |39-48| = 7 + 9 = 16
The closest is [35, 48]. Let me try to navigate toward it. From [42, 39], I need to go UP and RIGHT.
But first, let me check: what does SPACE do? Let me try it.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Player is the blue cross, moves 3 cells per action in any cardinal direction. Current center at [42,39]. Blue targets at [16,48], [24,40], [24,53], [35,48]. Yellow targets at [3,15], [9,6], [9,24], [17,15]. Yellow cross at [27,21].
Action model: Each arrow key moves the cross 3 cells. SPACE function unknown - likely interact/collect.
Goal: Collect the blue targets (gray squares with blue centers) by overlapping the cross with them.
Plan: Test SPACE, then navigate to collect targets.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me first check if any blue targets are currently within the cross's area
# Cross center at [42, 39], arms extend ~13 cells each direction
# Cross covers: vertical bar at col 39, rows 26-56; horizontal bar at row 42, cols 26-52

# Blue targets: [16,48], [24,40], [24,53], [35,48]
# None seem to be directly on the cross bars currently

# Let me check what the cross looks like in detail
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] > 1:
        print(f"Blue bar {n['id']}: pixels={n['pixels']}, boundary={n['boundary']}")

# The cross arms are 13 pixels each (from center to tip)
# Let me try SPACE to see what it does
predict(expect_change=True, note='SPACE might interact with nearby objects or trigger collection')
action(['SPACE'])

print(f"\nAfter SPACE: step={current_frame.step}")
print(f"Result: {last_action_result}")

# Check if anything changed
seg_after = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg_after['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'W':
        print(f"Player at: {n['boundary']}")
    if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] > 1:
        print(f"Blue bar {n['id']}: boundary={n['boundary']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
